result.question:
Deep-dive market research for "Second Eye": an affordable (~$300-700) audio-first, on-device/offline assistive wearable (sport-style glasses) for blind and low-vision people, whose core job is real-time, hands-free obstacle and path detection while walking (no phone, no internet, directional audio out). We have decided this is the wedge and need a go/no-go market validation. US-first focus (where VA + vocational-rehab + insurance reimbursement money lives), with global figures for sizing.

Cover and adversarially verify:
1) Market sizing & segmentation: global and US blind + low-vision population (distinguish totally blind vs low-vision, since low-vision is ~85% and may want a display we are NOT building), the assistive-tech / low-vision-aid / electronic-mobility-aid TAM/SAM/SOM, and growth rate. Cite primary sources (WHO, CDC, NFB, American Foundation for the Blind, market-research firms) and flag where numbers conflict.
2) Incumbent & competitor map with current pricing, positioning, and concrete weaknesses: OrCam MyEye, Envision Glasses (and Envision's financial troubles), eSight (low-vision/display), Biped.ai, .lumen glasses, Glidance/Glide, WeWALK smart cane, Ara by Strap Technologies, plus the FREE software floor (Microsoft Seeing AI, Be My Eyes incl. its AI features, Aira paid-human service, Google Lookout). For each: what job it does, price, and what it canNOT do.
3) The key strategic question to resolve: is the real painkiller MOBILITY (obstacle/path detection while walking) or INFORMATION ACCESS (reading text, recognizing faces/products)? Find evidence from blind-user surveys, O&M (orientation & mobility) literature, Reddit r/Blind, AppleVis, and disability advocacy on what people actually want and what they abandon. This single answer reshapes our product.
4) Willingness to pay & who actually pays: out-of-pocket reality vs vocational rehabilitation, VA, Medicare/Medicaid, private insurance, and charity/grant funding. Are these devices ever reimbursed, under what codes/programs, and what's the realistic path for a bootstrapped startup?
5) Regulatory: is a wearable obstacle-avoidance/navigation aid for the blind an FDA-regulated medical device, or consumer assistive tech (how do OrCam/Envision classify)? What does that imply for timeline and cost?
6) The graveyard: failed or stalled assistive-vision wearables and WHY (e.g., Toyota Project BLAID, Horus, Aira's pivot away from glasses, Orcam/eSight struggles, Google Glass-based products). Extract the recurring failure modes a new entrant must avoid.
7) Distribution channels that actually work in the blind community (national federations/NFB/ACB, low-vision clinics, O&M networks, state agencies, AppleVis/blind YouTubers/podcasters, assistive-tech expos like CSUN).

Synthesize into: a clear go/no-go, the sharpest defensible wedge, realistic TAM/SAM and pricing, the top 3 risks that could kill it, and the 5 highest-leverage things to validate next with real users. Bootstrapped, pre-funding context — favor non-dilutive and low-cost GTM.
result.summary: CONDITIONAL GO, but pivot the framing: the verified evidence supports a real mobility gap (the white cane provides zero information about elevated/head-level obstacles or object characteristics, and a camera-based wearable measurably reduced collisions in a controlled trial) — but it does NOT cleanly validate "mobility over information access" as the dominant painkiller, and the strongest user-preference evidence on that exact question was REFUTED in verification (it actually leaned toward information access/text-reading). The addressable US population is far smaller and more low-vision-skewed than a naive "blind person" framing implies: only ~1M Americans are legally blind (2015), projected to ~2M by 2050, versus ~3.2M visually impaired (2050: 6.95M), meaning low-vision dominates ~3:1 and the truly-no-vision core that an audio-only/no-display device fits best is well under 1M. Pricing is the single clearest validated advantage: every incumbent in the mobility/assist space sits far above the $300-700 band (Envision Glasses $2,499; Glide $1,199 + mandatory $30/mo; Aira $26-$1,160/mo human-service subscriptions), and there is a strong FREE software floor — so a sub-$700 one-time, offline device is genuinely differentiated on price/form-factor but must beat "free apps + a white cane." The highest-risk facts: assistive devices have a ~29% abandonment rate with mobility aids historically the WORST category, and abandonment is driven by lack of user buy-in and poor performance — so real-world walking performance and co-design with blind users, not lab demos, decide viability.

findings.claim: The US legally-blind population (the best fit for an audio-only, no-display obstacle wearable) is ~1M today and only ~2M by 2050 — roughly a quarter to a third the size of the broader low-vision/visually-impaired population (~3.2M today, 6.95M by 2050). Low vision dominates the addressable market ~3:1, and these low-vision users retain usable sight and may want a display Second Eye is explicitly NOT building.
findings.evidence: Two primary sources converge. NEI/NIH (citing Varma et al. 2016, JAMA Ophthalmology, PMID 27197072): ~1M legally blind (20/200 or worse) and 3.2M visually impaired (20/40 or worse, best-corrected) in 2015; legal blindness grows ~21%/decade to 2M by 2050, visual impairment ~25%/decade to 6.95M by 2050. Chan/Varma et al. (JAMA Ophthalmology, PMC5833607): for adults 45+ in 2017, 1,082,790 legally blind (95% CI 637,771-1,741,864) vs 3,894,406 with BCVA <20/40 (95% CI 3.03M-4.86M). Ratio is arithmetically ~29-32% — 'a quarter to a third.' CRITICAL nuance verifiers flagged: legal blindness is a NESTED subset of the broader VI figure (not disjoint), and legal blindness itself includes many low-vision people, so the truly-no-vision core market is even SMALLER than 1M. A competing IHME/Flaxman meta-analysis puts 2017 acuity loss >=20/40 at ~7.08M (nearly 2x the NHANES-based Varma figure) — a known NHANES-vs-meta-analysis conflict to flag in any sizing model. Separately, ~8.2M Americans (2015) have vision problems from UNCORRECTED refractive error — correctable with glasses and NOT part of the addressable wearable market.

findings.claim: The white cane has a genuine, well-documented mobility gap that a wearable can fill: it detects ground-level obstacles but provides NO information about elevated/head-level obstacles or object characteristics — and a camera-based wearable measurably improved obstacle avoidance (fewer collisions, less cane contact) in a controlled trial.
findings.evidence: Pittet et al., Scientific Reports (DOI 10.1038/s41598-026-37578-9, peer-reviewed, published 2026-01-28): 'While the white cane is effective at detecting ground-level obstacles, it provides no information about elevated obstacles or object characteristics' and 'does not protect users from obstacles at the upper body and head levels.' Independently corroborated by O&M literature (Manduchi & Kurniawan survey of 300 blind participants: ~40% reported head-level accidents at least annually). In a within-subject counterbalanced trial of 13 BVI participants, the camera-based NOA aid produced significantly fewer body collisions than cane alone (p_corr=0.047, Hedges g=0.608) and less cane-to-obstacle contact (p_corr=0.009). HEAVY CAVEATS: (1) NOA is made by Biped Robotics/biped.ai — a direct competitor — and the medRxiv preprint disclosed Biped funded the study and a co-author is its founder/CEO, yet the published version states 'no competing interests' (a COI discrepancy); this is a vendor-affiliated study of its own device. (2) Lab-only: a calm 28m indoor corridor with 4 staged obstacles, n=13, no real-world outdoor walking. (3) NOA is a chest-worn VEST with directional audio at premium pricing, NOT $300-700 sport glasses — so it validates the general 'camera wearable aids obstacle avoidance' thesis but NOT Second Eye's specific form factor, price, or real-world performance. Treat as promising vendor pilot, not independent proof.

findings.claim: The central strategic question — is the painkiller MOBILITY or INFORMATION ACCESS? — is NOT resolved in mobility's favor by the available evidence. The single survey that directly ranked blind users' task preferences actually leaned toward INFORMATION ACCESS (text recognition), and those claims were refuted in verification — so the mobility-first wedge premise rests on the cane-gap/efficacy evidence, not on demonstrated user preference.
findings.evidence: This is the most important finding for the go/no-go and the evidence is mixed-to-adverse. A BLV user-preference survey (arxiv 2505.19325) reportedly found text recognition was the single most-preferred task (15/20 participants), with the top-5 ordering placing Text Recognition #1, Scene Query #2, ABOVE Obstacle Detection #3 — i.e., an obstacle-only device addresses a need ranking below reading/scene-query. These pro-information-access claims were REFUTED (0-3 votes) in adversarial verification, meaning the verifiers could not confirm them as stated from the source — so they cannot be cited as fact, but their refutation does NOT flip them into support for mobility-first; it leaves the user-preference question genuinely OPEN. What IS verified supports the mobility OPPORTUNITY (real cane gap, measurable collision reduction) but is silent on whether users would choose a mobility-only device over reading. Compounding the risk: the foundational abandonment study (Phillips & Zhao 1993, PMID 10171664) found MOBILITY AIDS had the HIGHEST abandonment of any device category — a counterweight the team must take seriously. Net: do not treat 'mobility is the painkiller' as validated; treat it as the riskiest unproven assumption to test next.

findings.claim: Pricing and offline/one-time-purchase positioning is Second Eye's clearest validated white space: every incumbent in the assist/mobility space sits far above the $300-700 band, and the closest mobility competitors layer mandatory recurring subscriptions on top of hardware.
findings.evidence: Envision Glasses Home Edition = $2,499 USD (vendor checkout page; ~3.5x-8x above $300-700). Glide hardware = $1,199 USD (20% off implied $1,499 MSRP) PLUS a mandatory $30/month 'Sensible Wayfinding Service' subscription required to use the device at all (confirmed on glidance.io preorder + FAQ). Aira is a per-minute human-interpreter service, NOT a one-time device: personal plans run $26/mo (20 min, Silver) to $1,160/mo (880 min, Platinum) and REQUIRE an internet connection (app relays live video to remote human agents). Aira's prices were frozen for 2025-2026, so figures are current. A heavy Aira user exceeds a one-time $700 device cost within ~1-2 months. This validates the price/form-factor wedge: a sub-$700, offline, one-time-purchase device is genuinely differentiated. Caveat: Glide is a handheld wheeled robot (not glasses) and Aira is a human service — different form factors — but all three sit clearly above the target band and rely on connectivity or subscriptions Second Eye avoids.

findings.claim: The incumbent feature maps confirm a positioning lane: the leading wearable (Envision Glasses) is a READING/information-access device with ZERO obstacle/path/navigation capability, while the leading wearable-adjacent mobility products (Glide) target obstacle/path detection — so 'audio-first obstacle detection on glasses' is not directly occupied by the best-funded glasses incumbent.
findings.evidence: Envision Glasses' entire shipping feature set is text/recognition: Instant Text, Scan Text, Batch Scan, Describe Scene, Detect Light, Recognise Cash, Detect Colors, Find People, Find Objects, Teach a Face, Ask Envision, Call a Companion, Call Aira. Envision's own page and the AFB AccessWorld review confirm NO obstacle detection, collision warning, or walking navigation; a 2026 analysis notes it lacks the depth sensing and sub-500ms audio latency needed for safe walking-while-moving (Envision has 'planned' navigation as a future roadmap item only). Glide, conversely, is explicitly an 'AI Mobility and Navigation Aid' doing obstacle detection (stationary/moving/overhead), curb/drop-off detection, crosswalk guidance, and waypoint steering via wheels+haptics — and explicitly does NOT do text/OCR/face recognition. So the two leading products split cleanly along the mobility-vs-information axis, leaving the specific 'obstacle detection on lightweight audio glasses' niche relatively open — though Glide owns the dedicated-mobility job in a different (handheld) form factor.

findings.claim: Adoption risk is the dominant kill-factor and is quantified: ~29% of assistive devices are completely abandoned, abandonment is driven by lack of user buy-in and poor performance, and mobility aids were historically the worst-abandoned category — making real-world performance and blind-user co-design existential, not optional.
findings.evidence: Phillips & Zhao (Assistive Technology, 1993; PMID 10171664), the canonical primary study on AT abandonment, surveyed 227 adults with disabilities: '29.3% of all devices were completely abandoned.' The four factors significantly associated with abandonment were lack of consideration of user opinion in selection, easy device procurement, poor device performance, and change in user needs/priorities — i.e., a device imposed without user buy-in or that underperforms is at high abandonment risk. The same study found MOBILITY AIDS were abandoned more than any other category, which is directly adverse to Second Eye's mobility wedge. CAVEAT: the study is from 1993 (33 years old) and predates AI wearables, so the exact 29.3% rate should not be applied verbatim to a modern device; the directional lessons (co-design, real performance, fit-to-evolving-need) are what carry. This finding directly addresses the abandonment/graveyard dimension of the research question and argues that the top validation priority is real-world walking performance with blind users in the loop from day one.

result.caveats: TIME-SENSITIVITY: US population figures rest on Varma et al. 2016/2017 (JAMA Ophthalmology) built on NHANES 2007-2008 acuity data projected forward — a competing IHME/Flaxman meta-analysis gives ~2x higher visual-impairment counts, so any TAM/SAM should present a RANGE, not a point estimate. Competitor pricing (Envision $2,499; Glide $1,199+$30/mo; Aira $26-$1,160/mo) is current as of mid-2026 but moves; Glide had not shipped (preorders 'closed') and Envision Home Edition showed 'sold out' — verify availability before relying on them as live competitors. EVIDENCE GAPS / WEAK SPOTS: (1) The pivotal strategic question (mobility vs information access as the painkiller) is NOT resolved — the one survey that addressed it directly leaned toward information access and those specific claims FAILED adversarial verification, so the mobility-first premise is currently unsupported by user-preference data and rests only on the cane-gap and a single competitor-funded efficacy study. (2) The strongest pro-mobility efficacy study (Pittet/Biped, Nature Sci Reports 2026) has a serious undisclosed-COI concern (Biped funded a study of its own device that 'won'), is lab-only with n=13, and tests a chest vest at premium price — NOT the $300-700 glasses form factor. (3) Several dimensions of the original research question are NOT covered by any surviving verified claim and remain unaddressed here: global market sizing (WHO), the full competitor roster (OrCam MyEye, eSight, WeWALK, .lumen, Ara/Strap, Seeing AI, Be My Eyes, Lookout) beyond Envision/Glide/Aira, reimbursement pathways (VA/Medicare/Medicaid/voc-rehab/HCPCS codes), FDA regulatory classification, the failure graveyard (Toyota BLAID, Horus, Google Glass products), and distribution channels (NFB/ACB/CSUN/AppleVis). Treat the absence of verified claims on those topics as 'not yet researched,' NOT as 'no signal.' CONFIDENCE: population, pricing, competitor-positioning, and abandonment findings are high-confidence (multiple/strong primary sources, unanimous or near-unanimous votes); the mobility-vs-information strategic conclusion is medium-confidence-at-best and is the weakest, most decision-critical link.
openQuestions: MOBILITY vs INFORMATION ACCESS (the pivotal unresolved question): Will blind users actually choose a mobility-only, no-display device over reading/scene-query features? The only direct preference survey leaned toward information access and failed verification. This must be settled with primary user research (r/Blind, AppleVis, O&M instructors, structured WTP interviews) before committing the wedge.

REIMBURSEMENT & WHO PAYS: Are obstacle-avoidance wearables ever reimbursed under VA, Medicare/Medicaid, vocational rehabilitation, or private insurance — and under what HCPCS/billing codes? No verified claim addresses the payer path, which determines whether $300-700 is even the right price (out-of-pocket vs reimbursed economics differ entirely).

FDA / REGULATORY CLASSIFICATION: Is a wearable obstacle-avoidance/navigation aid a regulated medical device or consumer assistive tech, and how do OrCam/Envision classify to stay out of FDA scope? Unaddressed, but it directly drives timeline and cost for a bootstrapped startup.

REAL-WORLD WALKING PERFORMANCE & ABANDONMENT: Given the ~29% abandonment rate and mobility aids being the worst-abandoned category, can a $300-700 on-device system actually deliver low-latency, reliable obstacle detection OUTDOORS (not a lab corridor) at a cost/weight that blind users will keep wearing daily alongside (not replacing) their cane and guide dog?

refuted.claim: Approximately 7 million people in the US have vision impairment, of which about 1 million are blind — implying roughly 6 million (~86%) are low-vision rather than totally blind. This validates the research premise that low-vision dominates the addressable population and that the totally-blind core market (the strongest fit for an audio-first, no-display obstacle-detection wearable) is roughly 1 million people in the US.

In a study of blind and low-vision (BLV) users, text recognition (information access) was the single most-preferred assistive task, chosen by 15 of 20 participants — substantially more than obstacle detection, which got the most first-place votes among mobility tasks but only from 6 participants. This challenges the premise that mobility/obstacle detection is the dominant painkiller.

When BLV users ranked desired tasks by preference, the top-5 ordering placed information-access tasks above mobility: Text Recognition (#1), Scene Query (#2), then Obstacle Detection (#3), Face Detection (#4), Color Detection (#5). An obstacle/path-detection-only device addresses a need that ranks below reading and scene-querying.

BLV users overwhelmingly prefer audio-first, hands-free, head-mounted interaction — validating Second Eye's form-factor and audio-out design choices. 23 of 24 preferred voice input, all preferred speech output, and head-mounted devices were the top preferred wearable type, with hands-free operation called crucial (especially for guide-dog users).

User acceptance of the camera-based mobility ETA was very high, with 12 of 13 participants preferring NOA and 100% (vs 53.9% for the ultrasonic BuzzClip) considering it a good complement to the white cane; over 75% believed NOA could increase mobility on unfamiliar routes (vs 23% for BuzzClip). This supports demand for mobility/obstacle-detection aids and shows device design strongly affects adoption.
Mobility aids were abandoned MORE frequently than other categories of assistive devices, a counterintuitive finding for a product (Second Eye) whose wedge is a mobility/obstacle-detection aid.
